frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Rohit: Mummy, I know office mein practical issues hote hain, but pata nahi, mangalsutra na pehne toh thoda ajeeb lagta hai... shaadi-shuda aurat par woh accha bhi lagta hai, proper sa.
Mother: Haan beta, roz ka roz nahi toh at least office jaate waqt pehen le. Aajkal ki ladkiyan isko bas jewellery samajhti hain, par yeh apni parampara ka hissa hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a married woman's visible marital marker as part of how she should properly look and ties compliance to tradition rather than her comfort. It encodes disapproval when a married woman does not wear a mangalsutra consistently and uses cultural pride to mask control.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 31. Married for one year. A man in Mumbai. Your wife Neha told you last night that she wants to stop wearing her mangalsutra to work because it gets in the way and she finds it uncomfortable during long hours at her desk. She is not saying she wants to remove it permanently Ñ just not wear it to office. You said you would think about it. You are now on a phone call with your mother telling her what Neha said and asking your mother what she thinks. You are not asking for permission Ñ you are asking for support for the discomfort you felt when Neha said it and you cannot fully explain why it bothered you as much as it did.
